risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Random assignment was performed by means of numbered cards in envelopes for the intervention and the control group, and was stratified by age group (<, ⩾60 years)." Comment: This describes a randomized allocation process with stratification and sealed/enveloped allocation, which is sufficient to suggest a proper random method was used even though the exact random-number mechanism is not stated.

Quote: "Random assignment was performed by means of numbered cards in envelopes for the intervention and the control group" Comment: This is the only explicit allocation procedure described. Although the paper does not say "opaque" or "sealed," the use of numbered cards in envelopes indicates a pre-prepared envelope-based allocation process intended to prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment; no contrary description (eg, open list, alternation) is given.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Blinding was not possible within the hospital procedures. Nonetheless, in most cases physicians did not know in which arm patients had been randomised." Comment: Participants were necessarily aware of receiving the decision aid intervention versus standard care, and personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded. Because the study also assessed subjective outcomes and treatment-planning behaviors (e.g., decisional conflict, perceived involvement, satisfaction), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes. The partial physician blinding does not remove the clear risk from unblinded participants/personnel overall.

Quote: "Blinding was not possible within the hospital procedures. Nonetheless, in most cases physicians did not know in which arm patients had been randomised." and "Patients were assessed ... at 1 week follow-up. ... For completion of the follow-up, patients received a pre-paid envelope to send back the questionnaire." Comment: The main outcomes included patient-reported decisional conflict, perceived involvement in care, satisfaction, length of consultation, and time point of decision making, all of which rely on self-report and therefore on the participant as assessor. The trial explicitly states that blinding was not possible, so knowledge of allocation could influence these subjective outcome measurements. Although treatment uptake was taken from charts (objective), the presence of multiple subjective outcomes means the domain is judged on the most vulnerable outcome, which is high risk.

Summary: Of 152 randomized participants, the final analysed sample was 111. The paper states, "The final sample was 111 patients, of whom 55 were randomly assigned to the intervention group and 56 to the control group. Here, 13 and 16 patients respectively were excluded from the analyses because of post hoc ascertainment of not having met the inclusion criteria." Comment: This indicates substantial post-randomization exclusions/losses before analysis (41/152 = 27.0% not in the final sample), and the exclusions were not trivial. Although the exclusion counts were somewhat balanced between arms (13 vs 16), the attrition/exclusion is still substantial and the report does not describe a convincing missing-data handling strategy (e.g., multiple imputation or mixed models) to rule out bias. Under the review rules, substantial attrition without a robust missing-data analysis is high risk.
